title: US Supreme Court rules geofence warrants require constitutional protections
titleZh: 美国最高法院裁定地理围栏搜查令需要宪法保护
author: cdrnsf
relevanceReason: 命中 2 个 AI/科技关键词。
summaryRaw: The US supreme court has ruled that law enforcement’s use of sprawling warrants that sweep up smartphone location data requires privacy protections under the fourth amendment, in a boost to critics who view their use as an unconstitutional dragnet.Justice Elena Kagan wrote the majority opinion, which held that the sensitive data scooped up by “geofence warrants” counts as a fourth amendment search, and offers individuals a “reasonable expectation of privacy”, even if they may be in a public area.“An individual has a reasonable expectation of privacy in records about his cell phone’s location, and police intrude on that constitutionally protected interest when they demand the information – even though for only a limited time, and from a third-party tech company,” Kagan wrote.The judges ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v US, against the government, in a case that has been widely viewed as a test of how privacy rights translate into a new digital era.The use of geofence warrants is widespread, and gives law enforcement agencies the power to compel tech companies to hand over sensitive cell phone data from people at or near crime scenes. The warrants allow police and the FBI to collect this information from individuals within the radius of a virtual “fence” during a particular timeframe. But they are not restricted to requesting data for precise targets.The Chatrie case focuses on local police’s pursuit of an armed bank robber in Richmond, Virginia. He fled with $195,000. Law enforcement tracked Okello Chatrie down through their use of geofence warrants. Chatrie had opted in to an optional Google “location history” feature that documented his location every few minutes. He was eventually sentenced to 12 years in prison, after pleading guilty.Chatrie’s lawyers argued that this search was overly broad and violated his fourth amendment rights, which protects individuals from “unreasonable search and seizure”. Lawyers said that police’s use of geofence warrants amounted to an official “search” under the fourth amendment, and didn’t meet the constitution’s requirements for one.The government had argued that accessing only a short amount of cellphone location information means this tactic does not count as a fourth amendment search and accordingly, should not be afforded the same privacy protections. But the judges in the majority disagreed.The judges in the majority opinion also wrote that the government’s characterization of generating location history as a voluntary choice is “meritless”.They suggested that people aren’t choosing to share private information with third parties and the government “just by doing the ordinary thing cellphone users do”. “The point of carrying smartphones is to use what is on them,” including the apps and services they provide – many of which use location data to customize a user’s experience, they said.“That argument ignores how and why Google users turn on location history: Google repeatedly prompts users to turn on the service, often warning that devices will not ‘work correctly’ otherwise, while not disclosing in that prompt how frequently users’ location information would be recorded, how precise it would be, or how it might be given to the government,” they wrote.Justice Sonia Sotomayor wrote that “even short-term monitoring” of a person’s physical movements can provide “a wealth of detail about [his] familial, political, professional, religious, and sexual associations”. She highlighted examples of a person’s trips to “the psychiatrist, the plastic surgeon, the abortion clinic, the Aids treatment center, the strip club, the criminal defense attorney, [or] the by-the hour motel”.Privacy advocates share her concerns that geofence warrants can be overly broad in the area they target, as well as the length of time they cover. “If the government doesn’t need to … link something to a crime, it could monitor a protest or an abortion clinic or a gun range or a church or an AA meeting or a doctor’s office,” Matthew Tokson, a law professor at the University of Utah, said.While the majority opinion noted that police conducted a fourth amendment search by accessing Chatrie’s location history data, they noted that the court of appeals will weigh in on whether the “search was reasonable, meaning that each of its steps was properly described with particularity and found to be supported by probable cause”.Law enforcement has said they need geofence warrants to find suspects and witnesses – after reaching dead ends. The US government, for its part, has argued that people can’t have a “reasonable expectation of privacy” when they are in public and have allowed a third party company, such as Google, to collect and analyze phone location data.The government noted in its legal filings that “only about one-third of active Google account holders actually opted into the location history service”; Chatrie’s lawyers noted in court documents that this amounted to more than 500 million Google users. Even Google has acknowledged in legal filings for the case that geofence searches “often run a high risk of sweeping in innocent users–sometimes thousands of them.” The tech company said that it’s common for these inquiries to cover private homes, apartment buildings, government buildings, hotels, places of worship, busy roads, and other locations that law enforcement hasn’t identified probable cause to search.The ruling marks the first time the US supreme court is considering the scope of the fourth amendment since a landmark 2018 privacy ruling. At the time, judges decided in a 5-4 decision that the government generally needs a warrant to track a person’s cellphone location history.Paul Ohm, a law professor at Georgetown University, said “today is a very good day for constitutional privacy.” He added: “The court reaffirmed that the police need a search warrant to turn private services like Google’s location tracking into a state surveillance tool.”

comments.author: js2
comments.textRawHtml:
From https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/06/court-rules-that-law-enfo...

Additional details:

> The information that Google provided to law enforcement officials came in three tranches. First, Google gave law enforcement officials a list of the 19 accounts (but without the names attached to those accounts) linked to devices that were within 150 meters of the bank during the 30 minutes before and after the robbery. Second, based on that list of 19 accounts, the government asked for additional information about nine accounts that were in the area during a two-hour period. At the third step, a detective asked for, and received, the names and information associated with three accounts – one of which was Chatrie’s.

> Relying on the location data, law enforcement officials obtained a warrant to search two residences linked to Chatrie, where they found almost $100,000 of the stolen cash, a gun, and demand notes.

> Prosecutors charged Chatrie with bank robbery. He asked the trial judge to bar prosecutors from using the evidence obtained as a result of the geofence warrant at his trial, arguing that the warrant violated the Fourth Amendment.

> A federal district judge agreed that the warrant in Chatrie’s case did not have the kind of probable cause and specificity that the Fourth Amendment requires. However, she nonetheless allowed the prosecutors to use the evidence, reasoning that even if there had been a violation of the Fourth Amendment, law enforcement officials had acted in good faith.

Link to ruling:

https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/25pdf/25-112_0am4.pdf
comments.textZhHtml:
从 https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/06/court-rules-that-law-enfo...

其他详细信息：

> 谷歌向执法官员提供的信息分为三部分。首先，谷歌向执法官员提供了抢劫前后 30 分钟内与银行 150 米范围内的设备关联的 19 个帐户（但没有附加这些帐户的名称）的列表。其次，根据 19 个帐户的列表，政府要求提供有关两小时内在该地区的 9 个帐户的更多信息。第三步，一名侦探询问并收到了与三个账户相关的姓名和信息——其中一个是查特里的。

> 根据位置数据，执法人员获得搜查令，搜查与 Chatrie 有关的两处住宅，并在那里发现了近 10 万美元的被盗现金、一把枪和缴款单。

> 检察官指控查特里抢劫银行。他要求主审法官禁止检察官在审判中使用通过地理围栏搜查令获得的证据，认为该搜查令违反了第四修正案。

> 一位联邦地区法官同意查特里案件中的逮捕令不具备第四修正案所要求的可能原因和具体性。然而，她仍然允许检察官使用证据，理由是即使存在违反第四修正案的情况，执法人员也是善意行事的。

裁决链接：

https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/25pdf/25-112_0am4.pdf

children.author: petcat
children.textRawHtml:
I guess don't bring your phone to a bank robbery.

I believe this is similar to how they nabbed the Washington State University murderer. The feds compelled Amazon to give them all the bluetooth MAC addresses that was seen by the Echo device in the home around the time of the murders and were able to correlate it to other devices their suspect's phone had been visible to.
children.textZhHtml:
我想抢劫银行时不要带手机。

我相信这与他们抓获华盛顿州立大学凶手的方式类似。 联邦调查局强迫亚马逊向他们提供谋杀案发生期间家中 Echo 设备看到的所有蓝牙 MAC 地址，并能够将其与嫌疑人手机可见的其他设备关联起来。

children.author: autoexec
children.textRawHtml:
> I guess don't bring your phone to a bank robbery.

You should also make sure not to bring your phone to anywhere where a nearby crime is happening because that's all it takes to make you a suspect and force you spend a bunch of money defending yourself. https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/google-tracked-his-bike...

Hopefully rulings like this make that scenario a little less likely to happen, but it doesn't stop it entirely, it just means that the police need to spend 15 minutes to get a rubber-stamped warrant before they turn everyone within a few miles of crime into a suspect.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我想不要带手机去抢劫银行。

您还应该确保不要将手机带到附近发生犯罪的任何地方，因为这会让您成为嫌疑人并迫使您花一大笔钱来保护自己。 https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/google-tracked-his-bike...

希望这样的裁决能够减少这种情况发生的可能性，但这并不能完全阻止这种情况发生，它只是意味着警方需要花 15 分钟才能获得橡皮图章逮捕令，然后才能将犯罪活动几英里内的每个人都变成嫌疑人。

children.author: sidewndr46
children.textRawHtml: one of the more fun things I learned during criminal court in Texas is that the absence of forensic evidence cannot exonerate an individual. The prosecutor and the judge covered that despite not having any forensic evidence, the jury would still be expected to be able to convict the defendant. If you weren't OK with that you weren't eligible to serve on a jury.
children.textZhHtml: 我在德克萨斯州刑事法庭上学到的更有趣的事情之一是，缺乏法医证据并不能证明一个人无罪。检察官和法官表示，尽管没有任何法医证据，陪审团仍有望对被告定罪。 If you weren't OK with that you weren't eligible to serve on a jury.

children.author: pseudo0
children.textRawHtml: They are trying to avoid a situation where you end up with one juror who watches a lot of CSI and insists that they need forensic evidence to convict, despite having a dozen eye-witnesses. If a juror cannot imagine a circumstance where the evidence could be beyond a reasonable doubt based on non-forensic evidence, then they aren't suitable to be a juror.
children.textZhHtml: 他们试图避免出现这样的情况：一名陪审员看了很多《犯罪现场调查》，并坚持认为他们需要法医证据才能定罪，尽管有十几名目击证人。如果陪审员无法想象基于非法医证据可以排除合理怀疑的情况，那么他们就不适合担任陪审员。

children.author: throwway120385
children.textRawHtml: For example, if you're sitting in your living room with a bunch of other people, many of whom know each other, and two people start fighting, you are all witnessing a crime and you can also all identify the two people fighting. It would be ridiculous to require DNA evidence in that situation.
children.textZhHtml: 例如，如果您和一群其他人坐在客厅里，其中许多人互相认识，并且两个人开始打架，那么你们都目睹了犯罪，并且还可以识别打架的两个人。在这种情况下要求 DNA 证据是荒谬的。

children.author: jjk166
children.textRawHtml: At the same time though, a bunch of people who know each other and the people allegedly involved could very easily share the same incorrect testimony. You wouldn't believe in bigfoot if 5 guys drinking beers swore they saw him while they were camping. Sending someone to prison or worse is much higher stakes. DNA evidence might be too extreme, but I'd expect some sort of evidence to back up a testimony. "What if the witness was wrong" just seems like always a reasonable doubt, or at least the number of witnesses who would need to corroborate something such that it ceases to be a reasonable doubt is impractically high.
children.textZhHtml: 但与此同时，一群互相认识的人和涉嫌参与其中的人很容易分享相同的错误证词。如果 5 个喝啤酒的人发誓他们在露营时看到了大脚怪，你就不会相信有大脚怪。将某人送进监狱或更糟的风险要高得多。 DNA 证据可能过于极端，但我希望有某种证据来支持证词。 “如果证人错了怎么办”似乎总是一个合理的怀疑，或者至少需要证实某些事情以使其不再是合理怀疑的证人数量多得不切实际。

children.author: SAI_Peregrinus
children.textRawHtml: It's a nitpick, but testimony under oath is evidence to US courts. All evidence is either testimonial or circumstantial.
children.textZhHtml: 这是一个挑剔，但宣誓作证 是 向美国法院提供证据。所有证据都是证言或间接证据。

children.author: pc86
children.textRawHtml:
Courts are not solving math equations and despite the popular belief to the contrary, most lawyers and judges are not bumbling fools. But to use math as an example of why you don't need an "impractically high" number of witnesses:

  - Let's assume "reasonable doubt" is 0.1%, so you need to be 99.9% sure someone is guilty before voting them guilty
  - Let's assume a random witness to a random crime has a 5% chance of getting some material fact wrong through no fault of their own
  - Let's assume that if you are on trial, there is a 20% chance you are guilty, based on the assumption if you're guilty and know you're cooked you're more likely to plea out, so the people remaining at trial are the truly innocent, the guilty who think they can beat the case, and the guilty who are just rolling the dice.
You still only need 3 witnesses telling the same story to reach >99.9% assurance of guilt. The odds of an innocent person getting convicted with 3 witnesses under this standard is 1 in 8,000.

In reality, witnesses are probably more than 95% accurate with material facts, especially when these are collected in isolation at different times, probably by different police officers.

And if we're being honest with ourselves, a lot more than 1 out of 5 people on trial are guilty of what they're being charged with. The bar for a DA to bring charges is very high, their entire careers are based on conviction rate.
children.textZhHtml:
法院并没有解决数学方程，尽管人们普遍认为相反，但大多数律师和法官都不是笨手笨脚的傻瓜。但用数学作为例子来说明为什么你不需要“不切实际的高”数量的证人：

  - Let's assume "reasonable doubt" is 0.1%, so you need to be 99.9% sure someone is guilty before voting them guilty
  - Let's assume a random witness to a random crime has a 5% chance of getting some material fact wrong through no fault of their own
  - Let's assume that if you are on trial, there is a 20% chance you are guilty, based on the assumption if you're guilty and know you're cooked you're more likely to plea out, so the people remaining at trial are the truly innocent, the guilty who think they can beat the case, and the guilty who are just rolling the dice.
您仍然只需要 3 个证人讲述同一个故事即可达到 >99.9% 的有罪保证。根据这一标准，一名无辜者在 3 名证人的见证下被定罪的几率为八千分之一。

事实上，证人对重要事实的准确率可能超过 95%，特别是当这些事实是在不同时间（可能由不同的警察）单独收集时。

如果我们对自己诚实的话，那么超过五分之一的受审者都对他们被指控的罪名有罪。地方检察官提出指控的门槛非常高，他们的整个职业生涯都取决于定罪率。

children.author: DiscourseFan
children.textRawHtml: Damn its almost as if juries exist to act as the sovereign so the violence which sustains the law can be vested in a general public that cannot be held accountable as a whole, similarly to how at least one member of a firing squad always has a blank.
children.textZhHtml: 该死的，陪审团的存在几乎就像是充当主权者一样，因此维持法律的暴力可以归属于公众，而公众不能作为整体承担责任，就像行刑队中至少有一名成员总是有一张空白一样。

children.author: jjk166
children.textRawHtml: Or perhaps the standards of evidence established for a pre-industrial society when eye-witness testimony was the best that could reasonably be achieved is not necessarily the optimal system for a digital society where everyone carries a high definition video camera in their pocket at all times.
children.textZhHtml: 或者，也许在前工业社会中，目击者证词是可以合理实现的最佳证据标准，并不一定是数字社会的最佳系统，在数字社会中，每个人都在口袋里随时携带高清摄像机。

children.author: DiscourseFan
children.textRawHtml: Yes a high definition video camera that is slowly becoming indistinguishable (and has always been subject to tampering regardless) from an artificially generated version. We never have anything beyond judgement.
children.textZhHtml: 是的，高清摄像机正在慢慢变得与人工生成的版本无法区分（并且无论如何都会受到篡改）。我们从来没有任何无法判断的事情。

children.author: broken-kebab
children.textRawHtml: But in what way do you believe these standards are inadequate? Video from phones is used in courts when available and admissible.
children.textZhHtml: 但您在哪些方面认为这些标准不够充分？如果可用且可接受，法庭会使用来自手机的视频。

children.author: expedition32
children.textRawHtml: This is why crimes used to have prosecution time limits. Nobody expected eye witness accounts to surface after 40 years. But DNA is eternal.
children.textZhHtml: 这就是为什么犯罪过去有起诉时限。没有人想到目击者的叙述会在 40 年后浮出水面。但DNA是永恒的。

children.author: DiscourseFan
children.textRawHtml: DNA is not eternal, but neither is anyone's memory trustworthy for that long.
children.textZhHtml: DNA 不是永恒的，但任何人的记忆也不会长久可靠。

children.author: cucumber3732842
children.textRawHtml: Prosecution time limits and those pesky rights people have is why so much stuff that would've been simple misdemeanors 70+yr ago is now "administrative" and "civil" law.
children.textZhHtml: 起诉时间限制和人们拥有的那些令人烦恼的权利是为什么 70 多年前可能只是简单轻罪的很多东西现在变成了“行政”和“民事”法律。

children.author: chpatrick
children.textRawHtml: I think the idea is to test (with the jury as a sample) if the evidence is compelling enough for society at large to agree with the verdict. It's rarely going to be 100% clear cut so the jury has to use their judgment.
children.textZhHtml: 我认为这个想法是为了测试（以陪审团为样本）证据是否足够令人信服，足以让整个社会同意判决。它很少会是 100% 明确的，因此陪审团必须使用他们的判断。

children.author: autoexec
children.textRawHtml:
> I think the idea is to test (with the jury as a sample) if the evidence is compelling enough for society at large to agree with the verdict.

Exactly. It's all about consent of the governed. We need the government to prove that they've got a valid reason to take someone's freedom before we let them do it. That's also why jury nullification is so important. It's another check on government to make sure that we feel that the laws we're being held to and the punishments we'll face when they are broken are acceptable.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我认为这个想法是测试（以陪审团为样本）证据是否足够令人信服，足以让整个社会同意判决。

确切地。这一切都取决于被统治者的同意。在我们允许某人这样做之前，我们需要政府证明他们有正当理由剥夺某人的自由。这也是为什么陪审团无效如此重要的原因。这是对政府的另一项检查，以确保我们认为我们所遵守的法律以及违反这些法律时我们将面临的惩罚是可以接受的。

children.author: notamario
children.textRawHtml: Jury nullification is a double edged sword. There are near countless examples of a jury determining a white man killing a black man isn’t a valid reason to take the white man’s freedom.
children.textZhHtml: 陪审团无效是一把双刃剑。陪审团判定白人杀害黑人并不是剥夺白人自由的正当理由的例子数不胜数。

children.author: estearum
children.textRawHtml: Eye-witness evidence: so unreliable it's an active insult that people gesture towards it as one of the pinnacle forms of evidence.
children.textZhHtml: 目击者证据：如此不可靠，这是一种积极的侮辱，人们将其视为最重要的证据形式之一。

children.textRawHtml: Reminds me of the Norm Macdonald bit where he says he would not convict someone based on DNA evidence. The punch line is something like "I'm not a scientist, what do I know about DNA?"
children.textZhHtml: 让我想起诺姆·麦克唐纳 (Norm Macdonald) 的言论，他说他不会根据 DNA 证据对某人定罪。其中的妙语是“我不是科学家，我对 DNA 了解多少？”

children.author: alwa
children.textRawHtml:
Was your prior assumption that forensic evidence must exist in every case—and that if it doesn’t, then there’s no way to convince a jury of someone’s guilt?

As in, as long as I clean up really well afterward, I can pretty much do what I want?
children.textZhHtml:
您之前的假设是法医证据 必须 每个案件中都存在这种情况——如果不存在，那么就没有办法让陪审团相信某人有罪？

比如说，只要我事后清理得很好，我就可以做我想做的事了？

children.author: sidewndr46
children.textRawHtml: I think you're missing the slippery slope that this goes down. The criminal charges were way too low, given the alleged actions. The state admitted it had absolutely no forensic evidence. The judge was perfectly fine with this and selecting a jury that was OK convicting in this circumstance. This pretty quickly pretty us down a path of "you're guilty of at least one crime since you've been indicted, maybe a more serious one if we have some evidence".
children.textZhHtml: 我认为你错过了这件事的滑坡。考虑到所指控的行为，刑事指控太低了。该州承认完全没有法医证据。法官对此完全同意，并选择了一个可以在这种情况下定罪的陪审团。这很快就让我们走上一条“自从你被起诉以来，你至少犯有一项罪行，如果我们有一些证据，也许会犯下更严重的罪行”。

children.author: brookst
children.textRawHtml:
Does that mean that every single conviction from before the days of forensic evidence is necessarily invalid?

If the argument is that forensic evidence decreases uncertainty, well, it certainly doesn’t eliminate uncertainty.

Convicting anyone of anything is a slippery slope. The only way to be truly sure is to never do it, ever.
children.textZhHtml:
这是否意味着法医证据时代之前的每一项定罪都必然无效？

如果论点是法医证据减少了不确定性，那么，它肯定不会 排除 不确定。

对任何人定罪都是一个滑坡。真正确定的唯一方法就是永远不要这样做。

children.author: Spooky23

The standard of both investigations and the effectiveness of mostly part time police were pretty low. Most police were elected or appointed officials with deputized staff. The levels of training and professionalism were poor.

children.textZhHtml:
大部分兼职警察的调查水平和效率都相当低。大多数警察都是选举或任命的官员，并设有代理人员。培训水平和专业水平都很差。

你认为为什么很多地方都会处决小偷？抓到的人很少，一般来说，惩罚必须很严厉。

children.author: s1artibartfast
children.textRawHtml:
Evidence, not physical evidence. The instructions seem entirely reasonable.

There is no hard rule for physical evidence. If other evidence is enough to convince 12 jurors beyond a reasonable doubt, it could be very significant.
children.textZhHtml:
证据，不是物证。这些指示看起来完全合理。

对于物证没有硬性规定。如果其他证据足以让 12 名陪审员确信无疑，那么这可能非常重要。

children.author: Spooky23
children.textRawHtml:
Individuals don’t require exoneration in court.

The prosecution, regardless of unethical grandstanding, have a duty to prove the case without any resaonable doubt. They hold the burden of proof.

I would, of course, agree with them as physical evidence isn’t necessarily a requirement to prove a case beyond a reasonable doubt. But I would certainly draw my own inference from their mendacity if they used those words.

Your duty as a juror is to make a determination of fact and apply the law as guided by the judge to reach a verdict. When the prosecutor gives you some blabber approaching instructions, that’s an attempt to influence you.
children.textZhHtml:
个人不需要在法庭上要求无罪。

无论是否有不道德的哗众取宠，检方都有义务毫无合理怀疑地证明案件。他们负有举证责任。

当然，我同意他们的观点，因为物证不一定是排除合理怀疑证明案件的必要条件。但如果他们使用这些话，我肯定会从他们的谎言中得出自己的推论。

作为陪审员，你的职责是确定事实并按照法官的指导应用法律做出裁决。当检察官给你一些喋喋不休的指示时，这是试图影响你。

children.author: vkou
children.textRawHtml: Five (or fifty-five) people giving unambiguous eyewitness testimony that clearly identified the defendant and the crime he committed, with them all keeping their stories consistent under hostile cross-examination has exactly zero forensic evidence... but if you, as a juror, found all of that persuasive, it sounds like it should be enough to convict.

我的意思是……是吗？

如果有人犯罪，房间里有十个人说他们也在房间里，而且是那个人干的，那么这个犯罪嫌疑人不应该被扔掉，因为嫌疑人没有留下指纹。

At some point the absence of forensic evidence might look more like a deliberate attempt to keep exculpatory evidence out of reach of the defendant than a good faith attempt to prosecute.

children.author: stinkbeetle
children.textRawHtml:
I think you might be confusing what the issue is.

As we see in this case, the (likely) person in this case was actually found from the relatively small number of people who fit this criteria, so police were (likely) right to suspect these people.

The issue is not who the police may or may not suspect. it is about reasonable expectation of privacy. If they had obtained this exact same list of people who were near that bank for that time by a means that did not violate their reasonable expectation of privacy, then the evidence couldn't be challenged on that basis.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为您可能对问题所在感到困惑。

正如我们在本案中看到的，本案中的（可能的）人实际上是从符合这一标准的相对少数人中找到的，因此警方怀疑这些人（可能）是正确的。

问题不在于警察可能会或可能不会怀疑谁。这是关于隐私的合理期望。如果他们通过以下方式获得了当时在该银行附近的完全相同的人员名单： 没有 违反了他们对隐私的合理期望，那么就不能据此对证据提出质疑。

children.author: kube-system
children.textRawHtml:
I’m fully aware of the privacy concerns here; but that wasn’t the topic I was engaging in.

I’m saying that practically, being within a radius as the bird flies does not make you functionally “within the area” when there’s a huge z axis in a city.

When someone robs the retail of the ground floor of a high rise, there are many people who were right on top of the crime scene measured by lat/lon but were actually 10 minutes away by foot, hundreds of feet away in actual distance, and in no way aware it was even happening.
children.textZhHtml:
我完全了解这里的隐私问题；但这不是我正在讨论的话题。

我是这么说的 几乎，当城市中有一个巨大的 z 轴时，在鸟儿飞行的半径内并不会让你在功能上“在该区域内”。

当有人抢劫高层建筑底层的零售店时，有很多人就在经纬度测量的犯罪现场顶部，但被 实际上 步行 10 分钟，实际距离数百英尺，却根本没有意识到这一切正在发生。

children.author: ButlerianJihad
children.textRawHtml:
> reasonable expectation of privacy

That cracks me up honestly.

Imagine in the 1980s if every citizen was compelled to carry a ghetto blaster with 100W amplifier and speakers that relentlessly blared our SSN, phone number, home address and bank accounts.

In biblical times, of course, lepers were legally compelled to identify themselves and warn others with analogous methods.

Now today we all voluntarily carry around radio transceivers that loudly blare unique identifiers, perpetually, omnidirectionally, to anyone and anything that will listen.

Wired connections are so aggressively deprecated that we’re also perpetually exchanging every private conversation, and every secret SMS MFA code, and every DNS lookup, over public airwaves.

It’s not our fault and it’s beyond our control, but if you carry such a ghetto blaster, don’t cry to us about expecting privacy.
children.textZhHtml:
> 对隐私的合理期望

老实说，这让我很高兴。

想象一下，在 20 世纪 80 年代，如果每个公民都被迫携带带有 100W 放大器和扬声器的贫民窟爆破枪，无情地大声喊出我们的 SSN、电话号码、家庭住址和银行账户。

当然，在圣经时代，麻风病人在法律上被迫表明自己的身份，并用类似的方法警告他人。

现在，今天我们都自愿随身携带无线电收发器，这些收发器可以向任何愿意收听的任何人、任何事物永久地、全方位地大声发出独特的标识符。

有线连接已被大力弃用，以至于我们还通过公共广播永久交换每一次私人对话、每一个秘密 SMS MFA 代码以及每一次 DNS 查找。

这不是我们的错，也超出了我们的控制范围，但如果你携带这样的贫民窟爆破枪，请不要向我们抱怨想要隐私。

children.author: cucumber3732842

>A one hour period and 150 meter radius of a bank surrounded by high-rises and public transit? no.

Two weeks of no leads and that no is gonna start looking an awful lot like a yes.

两周没有任何线索，而“不”看起来会变得非常像“是”。

children.author: IncreasePosts
children.textRawHtml: There's no indication that this guy had to hire a lawyer or actually do anything. The same location data that put him near the scene/time of the crime would also absolve him. I guess it's sad that he felt the need to pay for a lawyer.
children.textZhHtml: 没有迹象表明这个人必须聘请律师或实际上做任何事情。使他位于犯罪现场/犯罪时间附近的相同位置数据也将使他无罪。我想他觉得有必要花钱聘请律师，这让他很伤心。

children.author: autoexec
children.textRawHtml:
Google told him that he would have to go to court to block the release of his identifying data to the police. He was not told what the request was about. At that time, he could only guess that it was related to the break in that happened near his home almost a year ago.

A lawyer at that point was a very good idea. Especially since all it takes is an arrest to cause you to lose your job and make it very difficult to get another one. It wasn't until after his lawyer got involved that the state attorney’s office contacted the police and told them this guy wasn't a likely suspect.

He would have used the same data Google already gave the police to win his case in court anyway, but it's a very good thing he managed to avoid having to deal with any of that before things went any farther.
children.textZhHtml:
谷歌告诉他，他必须上法庭阻止向警方透露他的身份数据。他没有被告知该请求的内容。当时他只能猜测，这可能与一年前他家附近发生的入室盗窃事件有关。

那时聘请律师是一个非常好的主意。尤其是因为只要被捕就会导致你失去工作并很难再找到一份工作。直到他的律师介入后，州检察官办公室才联系了警方，并告诉他们这个人不太可能是嫌疑人。

无论如何，他都会使用谷歌已经向警方提供的相同数据在法庭上赢得案件，但在事情进一步发展之前，他设法避免处理任何这些问题，这是一件非常好的事情。

children.author: autoexec

children.author: tsimionescu
children.textRawHtml:
Apparently, the legal understanding is that the Fourth Amendment doesn't guarantee some right that illegally obtained evidence can't be used against you (it merely guarantees that those obtaining the evidence illegally will be punished).

The reason why evidence obtained illegally is generally suppressed is to act as a deterrent to the Government. Even if individual officers were willing to risk their own punishment for illegal search or seizure (say, maybe they believe they are acting for the greater good), the evidence will generally be suppressed so that there is no rational gain from these illegal actions.

However, if the officers who obtained the evidence illegally were acting under good faith, then there is no deterrence obtained from suppressing the evidence they obtained. They did not act to illegally obtain evidence, in a way that they might be deterred from doing again if the evidence is suppressed - they thought they were collecting the evidence legally so they would do this again. So, in this case, there is no point in suppressing the evidence - no one is harmed by it being admitted (because, again, the Fourth Amendment doesn't promise you that illegally obtained evidence would not be used against you, it just promises that the Government will do all it can to avoid illegal search and seizure).
children.textZhHtml:
显然，法律上的理解是，第四修正案并不保证非法获取的证据不能用于对你不利的某些权利（它只是保证非法获取证据的人将受到惩罚）。

非法取得的证据之所以普遍被压制，是为了对政府起到威慑作用。即使个别官员愿意冒着因非法搜查或扣押而受到惩罚的风险（例如，也许他们认为自己是为了更大的利益而行动），证据通常也会被压制，因此这些非法行为不会获得合理的收益。

然而，如果非法获取证据的官员是出于善意行事，那么压制他们所获取的证据就不会产生威慑力。他们并没有采取非法获取证据的方式，如果证据被压制，他们可能会被阻止再次这样做 - 他们认为他们正在合法收集证据，所以他们会再次这样做。因此，在这种情况下，压制证据是没有意义的——没有人会因为承认证据而受到伤害（因为，第四修正案并没有向你保证非法获得的证据不会被用来对付你，它只是承诺政府将尽一切努力避免非法搜查和扣押）。

children.author: tsimionescu
children.textRawHtml:
You have to consider the fact that it's really weird for everyone to know that evidence against you exists, but be forced to pretend it doesn't and hide it from the jury. It's not at all self-evident that this is the only, or the best possible, remedy against the government abusively collecting this evidence from you. Ultimately it's very important for the good functioning of society that guilty parties are appropriately punished and innocent parties are exonerated, and disregarding evidence can harm both goals. It's of course also important that the government doesn't seize your property or violate your privacy without a reasonable cause, but this doesn't necessarily conflict with the primary goal of justice.

Also, the good faith exception is supposed to be relatively weak - it's supposed, at least originally, to only apply in cases where the officers performing the illegal action had no reasonable way of knowing that it is illegal; the original case is quite clear - a search was conducted in one state based on an out-of-state warrant, and that warrant itself was later deemed to have been improperly issued. I find it quite reasonable to say that the officers conducting the search had no reasonable way of knowing that this warrant was problematic. If the good faith doctrine was watered down so much that an "oops, I didn't know I was breaking the law" from an officer is enough, then the problem lies with these standards, not with the principle.
children.textZhHtml:
你必须考虑这样一个事实，即每个人都知道不利于你的证据存在，但却被迫假装不存在并向陪审团隐瞒，这真的很奇怪。这是针对政府滥用从您那里收集这些证据的唯一或最好的补救措施，这根本不是不言而喻的。最终，有罪的一方受到适当的惩罚，无辜的一方被无罪释放，这对于社会的良好运转非常重要，而忽视证据可能会损害这两个目标。当然，政府不会在没有合理原因的情况下没收你的财产或侵犯你的隐私也很重要，但这并不一定与正义的主要目标相冲突。

此外，善意例外应该相对较弱——至少在最初，它应该只适用于实施非法行为的官员没有合理方式知道该行为是非法的情况；原案非常清楚 -- -- 根据州外搜查令在一个州进行搜查，而该搜查令本身后来被认为签发不当。我认为可以合理地说，进行搜查的官员没有合理的方式知道该搜查令有问题。如果诚信原则被淡化得如此严重，以至于一名官员说“哎呀，我不知道我违反了法律”就足够了，那么问题就出在这些标准上，而不是原则上。

children.author: qingcharles
children.textRawHtml:
This is mostly true.

You have to remember that evidence exclusion for a constitutional violation is a modern thing, and it is what's known as "judge made," e.g. it wasn't made by legislature, it was invented by the courts. (Miranda warnings are the same -- I remember one time-travel book I was reading where the guy went back to 19th century New York and was complaining about the police beating him and not reading him his rights)

So sometimes it can be kinda hand-wavy and bullshit, especially using the "good faith" exception which has been very over-used in the last decade or so, especially because of new technologies, which gives a get-out clause to the police unless the exact fact pattern of their "search" exactly matched some previous case that was solidified in appellate case law in their state or federal district, or by SCOTUS.
children.textZhHtml:
这基本上是正确的。

children.author: twoodfin
children.textRawHtml:
Because the police got a warrant, exactly as this decision now says was required.

And there's something called the "good-faith exception" for unreasonable warrants: If you get a warrant where it's required (or in this case, where the government tried to argue it wasn't!), and a magistrate grants that warrant, it's a legal warrant so long as all participants were acting in good faith, believing their actions to be legal. Even if a court later finds that the warrant should not have been issued for one reason or another.

This is why Alito was grouchy during oral arguments and in his opinion that the Court took the case in the first place. The police got a warrant, acting in good faith. It allowed them to identify the criminal, who was later convicted. It wasn't clear that any decision by the court on the warrant requirement would have anything but an advisory effect, and SCOTUS doesn't do advisory opinions by longstanding tradition.
children.textZhHtml:
因为警方获得了搜查令，正如该决定现在所说的那样是必需的。

对于不合理的搜查令，有一种所谓的“善意例外”：如果你在需要的地方获得了搜查令（或者在这种情况下，政府试图辩称它不是！），并且地方法官批准了该搜查令，那么只要所有参与者都真诚行事，相信他们的行为是合法的，那么它就是合法的搜查令。即使法院后来发现由于某种原因不应发出逮捕令。

这就是为什么阿利托在口头辩论中感到不满，并认为法院首先受理了此案。警方出于善意行事，获得了搜查令。这使他们能够识别罪犯，并随后被定罪。目前尚不清楚法院关于搜查令要求的任何决定是否只会产生咨询作用，而且 SCOTUS 也不会按照长期的传统提供咨询意见。

comments.author: ascotan
comments.textRawHtml:
The implications are far reaching beyond cell phones. any service that stores location data for it's user is subject to 4th amendment expectations _regardless_ of an opt-in. The court specifically rejected the argument that by opting-in the user is abrogating their privacy rights. If you centrally store location data you have an obligation to protect that data under the 4th amendment as private and would require a warrant.

The impacts here are with food delivery apps, fitness apps, weather apps, cloud services, ad tech agencies, data resellers/brokers, etc.
comments.textZhHtml:
其影响远远超出了手机范围。 任何 为其用户存储位置数据的服务受到第四修正案期望的约束，无论是否选择加入。法院特别驳回了用户选择加入即放弃其隐私权的论点。 如果您集中存储位置数据，则根据第四修正案，您有义务保护该数据的私密性，并且需要搜查令。

这里的影响涉及食品配送应用程序、健身应用程序、天气应用程序、云服务、广告技术机构、数据经销商/经纪人等。

children.author: seplox
children.textRawHtml:
> I don’t think a ruling about private records held by a private entity like google or a phone company naturally extends to surveillance of public places.

Even when the surveillance is being conducted by a private entity? A private entity that's selling access to its private records of the comings and goings of a sizeable chunk of the population to police who are buying specifically because it would be a 4th Amendment violation for them to collect the data themselves?

If it's reasonable for we consumers, who know that cell networks and phone makers are collecting our data, to expect privacy, then it's reasonable to extend that same expectation to operators of ALPR and related techs. There's no opt-out, after all. We can't reject the terms of service.

> 我不认为对谷歌或电话公司等私人实体持有的私人记录的裁决自然会延伸到对公共场所的监视。

即使监视是由私人实体进行的？一个私人实体将相当一部分人口的来往私人记录出售给警察，而警察专门购买这些记录，因为他们自己收集数据将违反第四修正案？

如果我们消费者知道手机网络和手机制造商正在收集我们的数据，期望隐私是合理的，那么将同样的期望延伸到 ALPR 和相关技术的运营商也是合理的。毕竟没有选择退出的余地。我们不能拒绝服务条款。

children.author: anigbrowl
children.textRawHtml: You are missing the point. It's not that Flock cameras are not intrusive (they are, I fully agree) but that your contract with the cell service company gives you some legal standing to claim you were injured. If your municipality erects flock cameras and you don't like them, you don't have standing in the same way because a) you are not a customer of flock with contractual rights that can be enforced, and b) elected officials are legally authroized to do things because they won political office via election. So unless you discover they were bribed by Flock or engaged in some other corrupt activity, you don't have the legal standing to complain about a policy outcome you dislike.

children.author: JoshTriplett
children.textRawHtml:
Observation: it is legal to listen to a conversation happening in public, and it is not typically legal to record it.

Some things that are not much of a problem at a small scale ("take a picture of a specific strange thing you see happening", "record one license plate of a specific car in relation to an incident") can become a problem at scale ("set up a video camera to constantly surveil the sidewalk and do facial recognition on it", "record every license plate that goes by and correlate your recordings with a million other people to generate a tracking map").

The problem is with pervasive surveillance, not discrete observation, and that's the spirit that laws about surveillance should attempt to uphold.
children.textZhHtml:
观察：在公共场合收听对话是合法的，但录音通常是不合法的。

一些在小范围内不是什么大问题的事情（“拍摄你看到正在发生的特定奇怪事情的照片”，“记录与事件相关的特定汽车的车牌”）在规模上可能会成为问题（“设置摄像机不断监视人行道并对其进行面部识别”，“记录每一个经过的车牌并将您的记录与其他一百万人相关联以生成跟踪地图”）。

问题在于普遍的监视，而不是离散的观察，这就是有关监视的法律应该努力维护的精神。

children.author: JoshTriplett
children.textRawHtml:
Depends on your local law, but in many, many places you cannot legally record a conversation you aren't a party to, even in public. In the US, in many states, it's not permitted to record a conversation you are a party to if others have not consented. There are various reasonable exceptions that permit recordings (e.g. public events, press conferences, trials, governmental meetings, recording interactions with police). "Conversation between two people happening in public" is not typically a permitted exception for recording.

Note that in this comment I'm talking about audio recording, which typically has much stricter regulations than video recording. I think the same principles should apply to video, and in some jurisdictions they do. But in my comment, I was using the laws around audio recordings vs physical eavesdropping to make an analogy about the problem of pervasive surveillance.
children.textZhHtml:
取决于您当地的法律，但在很多很多地方，您无法合法记录您不参与的对话，即使是在公共场合。在美国的许多州，不允许录制您的对话 是 如果其他人不同意，则一方同意。有各种合理的例外情况允许录音（例如公共活动、新闻发布会、审判、政府会议、记录与警察的互动）。 “两个人在公共场合发生的对话”通常不是允许录音的例外情况。

请注意，在这条评论中我正在谈论 声音的 录制，通常比视频录制有更严格的规定。我认为同样的原则应该适用于视频，并且在某些司法管辖区确实如此。但在我的评论中，我使用有关录音与物理窃听的法律来对普遍监视的问题进行类比。

children.author: simoncion
children.textRawHtml:
> Should they not be allowed to do that?

If that camera system is closed-circuit and its data is restricted to the premises they should be permitted to do that.

If the data from that camera system [0] can be removed from the premises by anything less than a search warrant or court order, then no, they should not be permitted to do that.

I know this isn't how the relevant laws work now, but they haven't been adequately updated to account for radical changes in the ability for companies to perform mass surveillance.

[0] ...whether raw or "processed" by -say- a "customer analytics" software... [1]

[1] Want a count of the day's customers? Check register receipts. Want to know what displays are most popular? Ask your employees, or employ someone to take notes. etc, etc, etc.

我知道这不是相关法律现在的运作方式，但它们还没有得到充分更新，以适应公司执行大规模监控能力的根本变化。

children.textZhHtml:
> 斯劳特认定，国会已将行政部门的控制权让给了行政部门。

国会通过的法律规定了解雇联邦贸易委员会委员所需的理由。

看来他们现在缺乏近一个世纪以来所拥有的权力。

或者阿利托新的“历史和传统”测试，它是为了消除堕胎而发明的，但现在被应用于国会所做的各种事情。

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml: Statutes don't supersede the constitution and passing one doesn't create that power.
children.textZhHtml: 法规不会取代宪法，通过法规也不会产生这种权力。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
> Statutes don't supersede the constitution…

"We can make rules the President has to follow" does not supersede the Constitution.
children.textZhHtml:
> 法规不能取代宪法……

“我们可以制定总统必须遵守的规则”并不能取代宪法。

children.author: galangalalgol
children.textRawHtml: It obviously depends on what rules they make. They can't make a rule creating a body of government employees that decide the substance of rules (not just implementation details), and then also has armed officers to enforce those rules, with its own judges to have hearings specific to those rules. Whether you heard ice or atf when you read that, they both fit. I like Gorsuch's opinion. He clearly calls out this danger of a half step of saying the president has complete control of the executive without also ruling the agencies themselves are unconstitutional. Realistically though instantly removing all those agencies would mean chaos. The court can't rule how to fix something, only that the rock brought before them is the wrong rock. The telling bit would be if someone then brings them a case where the removal of the ftc leadership has resulted in the agency not enforcing the laws as written. If they then side with the congress I would give them the benefit of my doubt. But I do also feel like their positions, while correct, are correct only out of the context of their environment.
children.textZhHtml: 这显然取决于他们制定什么规则。他们无法制定规则，创建一个由政府雇员组成的机构来决定规则的实质内容（而不仅仅是实施细节），然后还让武装官员来执行这些规则，并由自己的法官针对这些规则举行听证会。无论你读到这篇文章时听到的是ice还是atf，它们都适合。我喜欢戈萨奇的观点。他明确指出了这种危险的做法：半步就说总统完全控制了行政部门，却没有裁定这些机构本身违宪。实际上，立即取消所有这些机构将意味着混乱。法庭无法裁定如何修复某些东西，只是带到他们面前的岩石是错误的岩石。能说明问题的一点是，如果有人向他们提起一个案件，称联邦贸易委员会领导层被免职，导致该机构不执行书面法律。如果他们站在国会一边，我就会相信他们是无罪的。但我也确实觉得他们的立场虽然是正确的，但只有脱离了他们所处的环境才是正确的。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
> It obviously depends on what rules they make.

Today, the Court ruled that Congress can make the Federal Reserve an independent agency, but not the FTC. Same day, same justice!

What are the rules, exactly?
children.textZhHtml:
> 这显然取决于他们制定什么规则。

今天，法院裁定国会可以让美联储成为一个独立机构，但不能让联邦贸易委员会成为独立机构。同一天，同一个正义！

具体规则是什么？

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml:
I see and acknowledge your point. But going back to

>Slaughter determined that agencies congress had ceded to the executive branch had control of the executive.

Congress ceded FTC to the executive branch. Congress put the Federal Reserve in some magical land, outside the executive branch, that doesn't even make sense.

My theory was that SCOTUS ruled the executive had this power over the agencies executive branch. Seems SCOTUS doesn't want to touch federal reserve question with a 10 foot pole. But going back to my original theory, it is a slightly different framing, since everyone involved freely agrees FTC is an executive agency while the federal reserve does not enjoy this agreement.

I do agree the federal reserve as independent makes no sense but I don't think it's the same question posed since you're not starting with the assumption the agency in question is an executive agency. SCOTUS seems to have ruled that an agency in the executive branch has executive control, while not going so far as to determine that the federal reserve is in the executive branch which is an entirely different question.

It's important to note SCOTUS is too chickenshit to rule on anything but in the most narrow way possible. If you ask them to rule on something with a prior established fact that it's in the executive branch you're likely going to get a very narrow ruling that doesn't try to create a unifying theory of everything.
children.textZhHtml:
我明白并承认你的观点。 但回到

>斯劳特认定，国会已将行政部门的控制权让给了行政部门。

国会将联邦贸易委员会移交给行政部门。 国会将美联储置于行政部门之外的某个神奇之地，这甚至没有意义。

我的理论是最高法院裁定行政部门对机构行政部门拥有这种权力。 似乎 SCOTUS 不想用 10 英尺长的杆子来触及美联储问题。 但回到我最初的理论，这是一个略有不同的框架，因为每个参与者都自由地同意联邦贸易委员会是一个执行机构，而美联储则不享有这一协议。

我确实同意美联储作为独立机构没有任何意义，但我认为这不是同一个问题，因为你并不是从假设该机构是一个执行机构开始的。 最高法院似乎已经裁定某个机构 在行政部门 具有行政控制权，但不至于确定美联储属于行政部门，这是一个完全不同的问题。

重要的是要注意，斯科特斯太懦弱了，除了以尽可能最狭隘的方式之外，他无法对任何事情做出裁决。 如果你要求他们对某些事做出裁决，而这些事是由行政部门事先确定的，那么你很可能会得到一个非常狭隘的裁决，而不会试图创建一个统一的理论。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
> I do agree the federal reserve as independent makes no sense but I don't think it's the same question posed.

I think it's the core question; are there really rules at all?

The two rulings make that answer clear, I think.

Roberts in Cook says that firing was "out of step with the statute Congress enacted and our nation’s tradition of central banking protected from political interference". How is the FTC's setup in this regard not part of the same tradition? What part of the Constitution permits the Fed's existence outside of any of the branches? Why can Congress establish a central bank outside the Executive entirely, but not regulate the FTC?
children.textZhHtml:
> 我确实同意美联储作为独立机构没有任何意义，但我认为这不是同一个问题。

我认为这是核心问题；真的有规则吗？

我认为这两项裁决已经清楚地回答了这个问题。

库克的罗伯茨表示，解雇“与国会颁布的法规以及我们国家中央银行免受政治干预的传统不符”。 FTC在这方面的设置怎么就不是同一个传统呢？宪法的哪一部分允许美联储在任何分支机构之外存在？为什么国会可以在行政部门之外设立一个完全独立的中央银行，却不能监管联邦贸易委员会？

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml:
Slaughter says POTUS can fire commissioners in the executive branch. Cook says Trump isn't King and can only directly fire people actually in the executive branch and Trump wasn't able to prove the federal reserve was situated there.

Prove the federal reserve is in the executive branch, and that the ruling of Cook presumed it was, and then you have a point.

I fail to see the inconsistancy.
children.textZhHtml:
屠宰 说美国总统可以解雇行政部门的专员。 厨师 说特朗普不是国王，只能直接解雇行政部门的人，而特朗普无法证明美联储就在那里。

证明美联储属于行政部门，并且其裁决 厨师 假设是这样，那么你就说得有道理了。

我看不出其中的不一致。

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml: In this case, it did, that's why SCOTUS ruled against it.
children.textZhHtml: 在这种情况下，它确实如此，这就是为什么 SCOTUS 做出了反对的裁决。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
SCOTUS does not enjoy papal infallibility. They fuck up.

(Or act maliciously, as when Alito invented the new "history and tradition" test.)
children.textZhHtml:
最高法院并不认为教皇绝对正确。他们搞砸了。

（或者恶意行事，就像阿利托发明新的“历史和传统”测试一样。）

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml: Congress fucks up as well. There's as pretty strong argument that "independence" from the executive power vested in the POTUS and legislative branch puts that power in a place not authorized anywhere in the constitution, and for good reason, as the design of the constitution intends elected office to have direct control over powers of congress and the executive.
children.textZhHtml: 国会也搞砸了。 同样有相当有力的论点认为，总统的行政权“独立” 和 立法部门将这一权力置于宪法中未授权的地方，这是有充分理由的，因为宪法的设计旨在让选举产生的职位直接控制国会和行政部门的权力。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
Yeah, that's why we have the veto power; as a check on their power.

SCOTUS has now given the executive retroactive uncheckable vetos. Yikes. "Those rules we agreed to, signed into law, and followed for the last 90 years? HAHA PSYCHE SUCKERS!"

Reminder: On the SAME DAY, the SAME JUSTICE issued an opinion that the President can't fire a Federal Reserve member, in Cook, saying it was "out of step with the statute Congress enacted and our nation’s tradition of central banking protected from political interference". You're asserting consistency that simply does not exist; the Court is starting with the desired ruling and working backwards from there.
children.textZhHtml:
是的，这就是为什么我们有否决权；作为对他们权力的检查。

最高法院现在已给予行政部门追溯性的、不可核查的否决权。哎呀。 “那些我们同意、签署成为法律并在过去 90 年里遵循的规则？哈哈，心理傻瓜！”

提醒：同一天，同一正义发表意见，认为总统 不能 解雇库克的美联储成员，称其“与国会颁布的法规不符，也不符合我们国家中央银行免受政治干预的传统”。你所断言的一致性根本不存在；法院从期望的裁决开始，然后从那里开始倒推。

children.author: dmitrygr
children.textRawHtml:
> can't setup genuinely independent agencies

The US constitution lays out three AND ONLY three branches of government. The congress cannot create a fourth without an amendment. If they create an agency in the executive branch, by definition it reports to the head of the executive.
children.textZhHtml:
> 无法设立真正独立的机构

美国宪法规定了三个且仅三个政府分支。如果没有修正案，国会不能创建第四个。如果他们在行政部门设立一个机构，根据定义，该机构向行政主管汇报。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
> The congress cannot create a fourth without an amendment.

Sure. So explain the results of Trump v. Cook, which involve exactly that.

The same justice, on the same day, issued one opinion that says Congress can't put limits on firing FTC chairs, and another that Congress can put limits on firing Fed board members.
children.textZhHtml:
> 如果没有修正案，国会不能创建第四个。

当然。因此请解释一下特朗普诉库克案的结果，其中涉及的正是这一点。

同一天，这位法官发表了一项意见，称国会不能限制解雇联邦贸易委员会主席，另一项意见则称国会可以限制解雇美联储理事会成员。

children.author: dmitrygr
children.textRawHtml: Did you read the opinion? SCOTUS only said a process was broken by a step being missed. Trump can still fire Cook, just has to let Cook have a hearing about it. Nowhere that I see did they say Trump must at all consider what Cook says at said hearing or be bound by it in any way - only that she must be able to get a hearing. This does not seem to contradict his authority to fire her. Just like your job's HR will gladly give you a hearing about terminating you, even if their minds are all made up and nothing you say will change a thing.
children.textZhHtml: 你读过意见了吗？ SCOTUS 仅表示流程因错过一个步骤而被破坏。特朗普仍然可以解雇库克，只需让库克就此事举行听证会即可。我没有看到他们说特朗普必须考虑库克在听证会上所说的话或以任何方式受其约束 - 只是她必须能够参加听证会。这似乎并不与他解雇她的权力相矛盾。就像你工作的人力资源部会很乐意听取你关于解雇你的意见一样，即使他们已经下定决心，你说什么也改变不了事情。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml: Yes, I read both opinions, and am left wondering why "out of step with the statute Congress enacted and our nation’s tradition of central banking protected from political interference" applied to one and not the other.
children.textZhHtml: 是的，我读了这两种意见，但我想知道为什么“与国会颁布的法规不一致以及我们国家中央银行免受政治干预的传统”适用于其中一种而不是另一种。

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml:
SCOTUS ruled if something is in the executive branch, POTUS can fire the commissioner. Congress and everyone else explicitly is saying the FTC is in the executive branch, which puts it in a completely different question that regarding the federal reserve.

The ruling in Slaughter was not that president can fire commissioners no matter what branch they are in. It was never established in Trump v Cook that the federal reserve lies in the executive branch.

Your attempt here to falsely portray an inconsistency that doesn't exist. It's a different question as to whether the federal reserve is in the executive branch. You'll have to show the federal reserve is in the executive branch if you want the same ruling to apply or claim this inconsistency.
children.textZhHtml:
最高法院裁定，如果行政部门有问题，总统可以解雇该专员。 国会和其他所有人都明确表示，联邦贸易委员会属于行政部门，这使其面临与美联储完全不同的问题。

判决于 屠宰 并不是说总统可以解雇任何部门的委员。特朗普诉库克案中从未规定美联储属于行政部门。

你在这里试图错误地描绘一个不存在的不一致之处。 至于美联储是否属于行政部门，则是另一个问题。 如果您希望应用相同的裁决或声称存在这种不一致，您必须证明美联储属于行政部门。

children.author: ceejayoz
children.textRawHtml:
> It was never established in Trump v Cook that the federal reserve lies in the executive branch.

But that's insane, right?

"You can make an agency the President has no control over, but you can't make one the President has some control over."
children.textZhHtml:
> 在特朗普诉库克案中，从未证实美联储属于行政部门。

但那是 疯狂的， 正确的？

“你可以设立一个总统拥有的机构 不 控制权，但你不能让总统拥有控制权 一些 控制权。”

children.author: mothballed
children.textRawHtml:
If you ask me personally, I think the federal reserve as a non-executive non-congressional agency is insane, yes. But as a legal question I would hope the president has to establish the control he's exerting is actually in the executive branch before he tries to exercise constitutional powers of the executive branch that supersede contradictive law.

It would be pretty nuts if the president could just fire whomever he wants no matter what branch they are in based on a simple declaration he considers them to be in the executive branch.
children.textZhHtml:
如果你问我个人，我认为美联储作为一个非执行非国会机构是疯狂的，是的。 但作为一个法律问题，我希望总统在试图行使行政部门的宪法权力来取代相矛盾的法律之前，必须先确定他所施加的控制实际上是在行政部门。

如果总统可以根据他认为属于行政部门的简单声明就解雇他想要的任何人，无论他们属于哪个部门，那将是非常疯狂的。

children.author: ocdtrekkie
children.textRawHtml: I agree in general, but this is also why I say allowing authoritarianism through technicality. They know by punting to Congress, a body that is completely paralyzed, what the practical outcome of that ruling is.
children.textZhHtml: 我总体上同意，但这也是为什么我说通过技术性允许独裁主义。他们通过向国会这个完全瘫痪的机构下注，知道该裁决的实际结果是什么。

children.author: galangalalgol
children.textRawHtml: I agree that motive is likely in at least one justice. But at the same time, if they really wanted to get back to original principles, they would have to take a wrecking ball to virtually every agency without being able to provide any substitute for the load bearing bits. I think these artificially narrow rulings are what some of the justices think is the middle ground to work in that direction without bringing the roof down. Thomas in particular has advocated for simply taking out the walls and trusting congress and the states will somehow fix everything and it isn't their problem. I think his opinions have occasionally been horribly flawed, but I see his vision and get what he is hoping for. I suspect something like that is the only way a representative democracy could recur in the US. Right now, states with strong geographic bents towards authoritarianism can use power of the federal executive to strengthen their position. If the federal executive had no agencies and was powerless the way Thomas suggests, those states wouldn't have much impact. But that entails acknowledging the entirety of the federal bureaucracy is unconstitutional and creating all sorts of power vacuums. Who knows how that would turn out? I increasingly think it couldn't be worse than the likely end state of a federal autocracy if we don't.
children.textZhHtml: 我同意至少一位法官可能有这样的动机。但与此同时，如果他们真的想回到最初的原则，他们将不得不把破坏球带到几乎每个机构，而无法提供任何承载钻头的替代品。我认为这些人为缩小范围的裁决是一些法官认为的中间立场，可以朝这个方向努力，而不会让屋顶垮掉。托马斯尤其主张简单地拆除围墙并相信国会和各州会以某种方式解决所有问题，这不是他们的问题。我认为他的观点有时存在严重缺陷，但我看到了他的愿景并得到了他的希望。我怀疑类似的事情是代议制民主在美国重现的唯一途径。目前，在地理上有强烈威权主义倾向的州可以利用联邦行政权力来加强自己的地位。如果联邦行政部门没有机构并且像托马斯建议的那样无能为力，那么这些州就不会产生太大的影响。但这需要承认整个联邦官僚机构都是违宪的，并造成各种权力真空。谁知道结果会如何？我越来越认为，如果我们不这样做，情况不会比联邦独裁政权可能的最终状态更糟糕。

children.author: ryandrake
children.textRawHtml: One of Alito's main points appears to be that a search is not really a search, as long as it's laundered through a third party. Scary that this was 6-3
children.textZhHtml: One of Alito's main points appears to be that a search is not really a search, as long as it's laundered through a third party. Scary that this was 6-3

children.author: ocdtrekkie
children.textRawHtml:
I'm not too surprised because it's this court. I'm happy we can take the win for privacy rights.

We are very lucky we got this outcome out of this, because at least on its face the warrant was pretty well-behaved: They didn't get any identifying information until they had aggressively narrowed suspects. If someone had a convenient CCTV camera where the call was placed it all would have been moot. It's very possible if this geofence plan had only identified one person, the guilty party, it would be very hard to argue it was unreasonably broad as well. And as Alito did point out, this ruling won't actually get the thief out of his case.

We got a win today where a win certainly wasn't expected.
children.textZhHtml:
我并不太惊讶，因为这是这个法庭。我很高兴我们能够赢得隐私权。

我们很幸运能得到这样的结果，因为至少从表面上看，逮捕令表现得相当好：在积极缩小嫌疑人范围之前，他们没有获得任何识别信息。如果有人有一个方便的闭路电视摄像机来拨打电话，那么这一切都将毫无意义。如果这个地理围栏计划只识别了一个人，即有罪方，那么很可能很难说它的范围过于宽泛。正如阿利托指出的那样，这项裁决实际上并不能让小偷摆脱他的案子。

今天我们取得了一场胜利，这是一场完全出乎意料的胜利。

children.author: DetroitThrow
children.textRawHtml: She's not as big on some of the broader interpretations of the 4th amendment that more civil liberty minded justices would lend credence to.
children.textZhHtml: 她对第四修正案的一些更广泛的解释并不那么热衷，而更多有公民自由意识的法官会相信这些解释。

children.author: thewebguyd
children.textRawHtml: Particularly when it comes to tech, she usually goes along party lines but she's been surprisingly independent in other areas. When it comes to the 4th she does heavily prioritize the sanctity of the home and property rights.
children.textZhHtml: Particularly when it comes to tech, she usually goes along party lines but she's been surprisingly independent in other areas. When it comes to the 4th she does heavily prioritize the sanctity of the home and property rights.

children.author: TimorousBestie
children.textRawHtml: I have a pet theory that it’s difficult for her to convince the far right wing of the court to let her write the majority opinion, and that’s part of what is fueling these uncharacteristic or “independent” moments.
children.textZhHtml: 我有一个喜欢的理论，即她很难说服法院的极右翼让她写下多数意见，而这正是助长这些不寻常或“独立”时刻的部分原因。

children.author: newaccountman2
children.textRawHtml: I am pretty sure the Chief Justice chooses who writes the opinion when he (or, one day, she) is in the majority, and if that's right, then Roberts is the only one she would have to convince
children.textZhHtml: 我很确定首席大法官会在他（或者有一天，她）占多数时选择由谁撰写意见，如果这是正确的，那么罗伯茨是她唯一需要说服的人

children.author: s1artibartfast
children.textRawHtml: Iirc, they do a straw poll avter oral arguments to see what the majority is and most senior assigns. The writing is generally spread equally across justices over a term. It is a workload thing
children.textZhHtml: Iirc，他们在口头辩论后进行了一次民意调查，看看大多数人是什么以及最高级的分配。一般来说，在一个任期内，法官的写作内容是平等分布的。这是一个工作量的事情

children.author: ocdtrekkie
children.textRawHtml:
When the Court rules to the center, I think Roberts likes to take it himself or let a liberal Justice write it so it looks like the court is balanced and unified or something.

Roberts has lost control of his court and is desperately trying to make it appear legitimate.
children.textZhHtml:
当法院做出中间裁决时，我认为罗伯茨喜欢自己接受或让自由派法官来写，这样看起来法院是平衡和统一的。

children.author: ecshafer
children.textRawHtml: Calling part of the government illegitimate is a very slippery slope to justifying trying to overthrow said government. You can say they are corrupt, you can disagree with policies, but saying illegitimate is cause for extreme responses.

children.author: Edman274
children.textRawHtml: Do you always judge the validity of a statement according to the implications of it being true, or do you get to do it just this once as a treat? Further, how does saying a court is "corrupt" not call for "extreme measures" but referring to it as "illegitimate" does? What's the meaningful difference?
children.textZhHtml: 你是否总是根据某个陈述的真实含义来判断它的有效性，还是只做一次作为一种享受？此外，为什么说法院“腐败”就不需要采取“极端措施”，而说法院“非法”却需要采取“极端措施”呢？有什么有意义的区别？

children.author: ecshafer
children.textRawHtml: Calling members of a court corrupt, means you can pursue legal action against them, write laws, impeach, etc. calling the court itself illegitimate, implies the government that put them into power is illegitimate. Calling government illegitimate is the first move of revolutionary groups.
children.textZhHtml: 呼唤 会员 法院腐败，意味着你可以对他们采取法律行动，制定法律，弹劾等。称法院本身非法，意味着让他们掌权的政府是非法的。称政府非法是革命团体的第一个举动。

children.author: mjburgess
children.textRawHtml:
The CJ decides who writes the opinion of the majority if in the majority, and the dissent if in the dissent. Its the job of the CJ to bring sides together in clear oppositions, and "horse trade" between bits and pieces of a decision so that its clear where a majority/minority lie.

The CJ's foremost political role is to ensure the judicial branch of government is seen as a politically legitimate institution which wields its power against the other branches in a constitutionally and poltiically legitimate way. If that slips, congress can start hiring/firing; and the executive, in the end, controls the guns -- they can be arrested.

To avoid being arrested or fired, the court has to keep all sides believing the rules they set are fair.

They have no power, in the end, but the power they are allowed to have. They govern by consent of the other branches, and that's trivial to take away
children.textZhHtml:
如果是多数意见，则由首席法官决定由谁撰写；如果是反对意见，则由首席法官决定由谁撰写异议意见。首席法官的职责是将明确对立的双方聚集在一起，并在决策的各个部分之间进行“讨价还价”，以便清楚地表明多数派/少数派的立场。

最高法院最重要的政治作用是确保政府司法部门被视为政治上合法的机构，以宪法和政治上合法的方式对其他部门行使权力。如果出现这种情况，国会可以开始招聘/解雇；最终，行政部门控制了枪支——他们可以被逮捕。

为了避免被逮捕或解雇，法院必须让各方相信他们制定的规则是公平的。

他们最终没有权力，但他们被允许拥有权力。他们通过其他分支机构的同意进行管理，这很容易被取消

children.author: ocdtrekkie
children.textRawHtml:
I mean, this is the textbook definition of whataboutism. =)

But I am indeed of the inclination that we should demand the rule of law from Presidents of all parties. Generally speaking, I am in favor of a significant downsizing of the authority of the President as a whole. They have far too many powers and are granted too much leniency to use them "in case of emergency" which has increasingly just turned into every President declaring everything they want to do an emergency. Presidents should be subject to prosecution for misconduct, and upheld to the highest standards of the law, and we should have systems in place to swiftly and effectively remove them if they do not meet them. The bar for impeachment and removal is too high when it is unattainable in a two-party system where the President controls one of those parties.

Our country does not need kings of any party.
children.textZhHtml:
我的意思是，这是教科书上对“Whataboutism”的定义。 =)

但我确实倾向于我们应该要求各党派总统实行法治。总的来说，我赞成大幅缩减总统的整体权力。他们拥有太多的权力，并且被给予太多的宽大处理，以至于无法在“紧急情况下”使用这些权力，这越来越变成每一位总统都宣布他们想做的一切都是紧急情况。总统应因不当行为受到起诉，并遵守法律的最高标准，我们应该建立适当的制度，以便在他们不遵守规定时迅速有效地罢免他们。在总统控制其中一党的两党制中，弹劾和罢免的门槛太高了。

我们的国家不需要任何党派的国王。

children.author: ocdtrekkie
children.textRawHtml:
So very specifically I've historically read Roberts as a fairly moderate jurist. He has a true romanticism about the neutrality of the court and that it shouldn't be a political body. (This is ridiculous, but anyways.) This has changed as the court has reached a 6/3 bias. When the court was a 5/4, Roberts could swing to the center and bring the majority position with him. But now the far right wing doesn't need his help: The conservative wing can do a 5/4 even with his dissent. So you see Roberts bucking the conservative trend much less, maybe not because he agrees with the court but knows he can't push the outcome to the center.

The other aspect I think in play here is that the current executive branch pretty much just ignores every court order it doesn't like, and the Court can't enforce any ruling it makes, because that's the executive branch's job. I think Roberts knows if the Court pulls against Trump very hard, it could lead to a showdown where Trump just... does what he wants anyways, which would destroy the perceived power of the Court. I think Roberts has tried to dodge a lot of law and a lot of rulings to avoid clear positions on the President which he would, in turn, ignore.
children.textZhHtml:
因此，非常具体地说，我历来将罗伯茨视为一位相当温和的法学家。他对法院的中立性抱有真正的浪漫主义态度，认为法院不应该是一个政治机构。 （这很荒谬，但无论如何。）这有 改变了 因为法庭已经达到了 6/3 的偏见。当球场为 5/4 时，罗伯茨可以向中间摆动并带来多数席位。但现在极右翼不需要他的帮助：即使有他的异议，保守派也可以得到 5/4 的支持。所以你会看到罗伯茨对保守派趋势的反对要少得多，也许不是因为他同意法院的观点，而是因为他知道他不能将结果推向中间。

我认为这里起作用的另一个方面是，当前的行政部门几乎忽略了它不喜欢的每一条法院命令，并且法院无法执行其做出的任何裁决，因为这是行政部门的工作。我认为罗伯茨知道，如果法院非常严厉地反对特朗普，可能会导致一场摊牌，特朗普只是......无论如何都会做他想做的事，这将破坏法院的感知权力。我认为罗伯茨试图规避很多法律和很多裁决，以避免对总统的明确立场，而他反过来又会忽视这些立场。

comments.author: jmward01
comments.textRawHtml: So surveillance tech works. That isn't a question, it is a statement. In a perfect world only bad people would have it applied to them. The issue is that it is too easy now. When wire tapping became a thing it was a physical thing that had time and effort costs. Abuse of it was capped by capability to do it and that bar made it, to me, a reasonable compromise that a judge would need to issue a warrant and that the police would need to expend real, finite, resources to execute it. children.author: cmiles8
children.textRawHtml:
It’s raising the bar for doing these searches. Essentially saying some government investigator can’t go “oh well if we had this data we might find something interesting, so let’s get the data.” The court here is saying these geofenced searches smell a lot like such a fishing expedition hoping to find something interesting.

Rather you should have evidence that a specific person did a specific thing and need to conduct a search to find additional evidence of said person doing said thing.

The 4th amendment protects US persons from the government just doing generalized searches in hopes that it will turn up useful info. You have a right to privacy from the government unless the government can clear a high bar showing probable cause that you’ve done something wrong.
children.textZhHtml:
它提高了进行这些搜索的门槛。本质上是说一些政府调查员不能说“哦，如果我们有这些数据，我们可能会发现一些有趣的东西，所以让我们获取数据。”这里的法院表示，这些地理围栏搜索闻起来很像一次钓鱼探险，希望找到一些有趣的东西。

相反，您应该有证据表明特定的人做了特定的事情，并且需要进行搜索以找到该人做该事情的其他证据。

第四修正案保护美国人免受政府的普遍搜查，希望能找到有用的信息。您有权获得政府的隐私权，除非政府可以清除高标准，显示您做错事的可能原因。

children.author: deathanatos
children.textRawHtml:
They didn't rule on it because it is out of scope of the matter before the court. The lower courts first have to make a ruling that can be appealed.

From the ruling:

> The conclusion that a Fourth Amendment search occurred does not resolve this case, because the Fourth Amendment prohibits only searches that are “unreasonable.” When law enforcement officials undertake a search to discover evidence of a crime, the reasonableness standard generally requires that they seek a warrant from “a neutral and detached magistrate,” Johnson v. United States, 333 U. S. 10, 14, who may issue a warrant only when “probable cause is properly established and the scope of the authorized search is set out with particularity,” Kentucky v. King, 563 U. S. 452, 459. The warrant issued here, as described earlier, was an uncommon, multi-step one, and the parties have contested the legality of each stage of the search process it authorized. The Fourth Circuit did not address the questions that unusual warrant raises. Because this is “a court of review, not of first view,” Cutter v. Wilkinson, 544 U. S. 709, 718, n. 7, the Court leaves it up to the Court of Appeals to decide whether, at each step of the search process, the warrant satisfied the Fourth Amendment’s requirements of particularity and probable cause
children.textZhHtml:
他们没有对此做出裁决，因为这超出了法庭审理的事项的范围。下级法院首先必须做出可以上诉的裁决。

从裁决结果来看：

> 发生第四修正案搜查的结论并不能解决此案，因为第四修正案只禁止“不合理”的搜查。当执法官员进行搜查以发现犯罪证据时，合理性标准通常要求他们向“中立且独立的治安法官”寻求搜查令，Johnson v. United States, 333 U.S. 10, 14，只有在“合理确定了可能的原因并且具体规定了授权搜查的范围”时，法官才可以签发搜查令，Kentucky v. King, 563 U.S. 452, 459。如前所述，此处发出的搜查令是一种不常见的多步骤搜查令，各方对其授权的搜查过程的每个阶段的合法性都存在争议。第四巡回法院没有解决不寻常搜查令提出的问题。因为这是“复审法院，而不是初审法院”，Cutter v. Wilkinson, 544 U.S. 709, 718, n. 7、法院让上诉法院在搜查过程的每一步中决定搜查令是否满足第四修正案关于特殊性和可能原因的要求

comments.textRawHtml: rare scotus W, but i strongly suspect that because this data is "owned" by someone other than the people that generated it that said owners will simply choose to voluntarily cooperate with government inquiries 100% of the time.
